item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
She helped me up, laughed an easy laugh, and have me a beer and an ice pack.

should that be "gave me a beer" instead of "have me a beer"? i'd normally fix minor typos but this is in dialog and might be intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given the expectation of something horrible befalling personnel expressing sentiment in the world of the Foundation, I thought the ending was great. Really dug that tension and the ideas regarding violence and culture throughout.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I've really tried to reduce the number of times I upvote over a single line before finishing an article, but this:

offenders receive less daily violence overall

Absolutely did me in, and I'm grateful I didn't have to look back.

If I had any complaints, it would be that there doesn't seem to be a justification for splitting up the audio log. It works beautifully, pacing-wise, but it tugged at my suspension-of-disbelief a touch.

Regardless, I adored the worldbuilding. Though it continues to be comical throughout, it's also thoughtfully deepened and inspected. That the exchanges in the dialogue felt genuine (helped in no small part by a steady dose of levity) without being overbearing and emotional, was a nice bonus.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
upvoted immediately after "weekend classes are available" and didn't regret it one bit. i love the camaraderie between the two officers (especially when lowen asked wentz to be his best man)

(extra +1 for letterkenny in the authorpost)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's totally fair!

I subscribe to the "there is no canon" mentality, holding fast only to the tenants of secrecy, clinical procedure and containment in their most basic terms. Some other considerations have to be made for writing the Foundation as a character, such as an aversion to terminating anomalies, but for the most part the benevolent v. bottom line Foundation argument isn't one for me; I hop over that line all the dang time.

To me, the Foundation would accept that all of humanity, by virtue of existence, is somewhat anomalous. Normalcy is relative. The citizens of White Brook may be "different", sure, but they're close enough to baseline humanity that it doesn't really matter. You can also consider it two ways; either the population can be considered a broad-strokes Thaumiel tool for containment, or they're just slightly different people who've proven their loyalty and humanity over a CENTURY of cooperation.

Thanks for the read regardless! Glad it was fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really wanted to give this an upvote, because it really is a very fun article.
Unfortunately everyone's behavior is so out of character for the Foundation. Accepting White Brooks citizens, who are essentially SCPs, into the Foundation despite this procedure by their own admission resulting in a significant change to the lifestyle/population of the town and a detriment to personnel cohesion and their physical integrity. Letting them in on things that are supposed to be super secret for no real reason, despite no real advantage and many disadvantages, seems like the kind of thing you'd see in a lighter, more comedic parody of the Foundation.
Not getting a downvote either though, since like I said, this IS hella fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks so much for the review!

The intention of this article was to be an anti-climax, with the perceived comical violence and anomalous physiology alluding to some kind of inhuman brutality. I'm sorry the reversal/reveal of the town being both sensible, sane, and a place where a lonely man can find love didn't hit properly for you. You're also right, it does stray heavily from the traditional scientific article format.

As I've said before, I'm working to incorporate my writing style into the SCP format. I appreciate the communities patience and support :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the read! It's fun exploring the Foundation from different potential angles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was sweet without being cloying, and had some amusing lines, but to me, it really just fails to have any pay off. More egregiously, the only part of the SCP which is clearly anomalous, the symbiotic organism present in the residents, is not discussed at all, causing this to read like a supplement without an article. I couldn't tell you how to properly address that aspect of the anomaly without throwing off the whole tone and pacing of the story, but I don't like that, in a format that at it's heart is supposed to be a scientific document, that part is hastily swept aside for the story you really wanted to tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just love some of the more light-hearted SCP articles. And I do like it when the foundation isn't pointlessly evil. After all. While they may do horrible things all the time, it's good to see times when (since it isn't necessary) they don't do something cruel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed it!

I'm still working to translate my style of writing to the SCP format. Some of my articles may end up reading more like short stories than I'd like. I'm just glad the community has had the patience to deal with it. Hopefully my future SCPs will be more, well, SCP-like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is just me or has this series had a lot more of these longer, less formal story scips? If I'm being honest this isn't really an SCP, like I can't imagine the foundation actually writing this as a document, but man does it work for what it is.

+1, but I kinda wish there was a separate category somewhere between a SCP and a Tale.

item.title: Re: A few notes:
item.content:encoded:
Thanks so much silverpanther! Both of those issues have been corrected; good eye.

As for Super Soft Birthday Parties, they're highly encouraged. That said, marijuana hasn't been legalized in White Brook yet, so Joint Boy might not get an invite.

item.title: A few notes:
item.content:encoded:
T, you're easily my newest favorite author on this site, but there were a few things I wanted to point out that stuck out to me in this article:

Wentz' first line in Evidence Log 2/4 reads: "How are going to keep this a secret?" and I believe a 'you' has a place somewhere in that line.

In the description, the bullet point "A 10-15 higher bpm" didn't really make a lot of sense to me without the footnote. I think something like "A heart rate 10-15 bpm higher than baseline" would flow a lot better, and would negate the need for the footnote altogether.

Beyond that, I felt just as taken by this article as all your previous ones. And if that didn't secure a +1 from me, quoting the Letterkenny cold open in your author post absolutely did the trick. I'm very curious about White Brook's traditions regarding Super Soft Birthday Parties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: White Brook welcomes you. Your welcome basket of knuckle-wrappings and DVD copy of Fight Club will be on your doorstep when you arrive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Found where I'm moving to.

+1 for bear suplex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCPs don't always need to be horror and tragedy! Glad you enjoyed the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i can't remember the last time i read an SCP about a town-wide anomaly that had an objectively happy ending. i'm glad i read this one. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah! I am. I'd originally had him written into Vulcan, the Disaster as the original site director, but the reference felt too on the nose. So instead I tossed him in here. Thanks so much for the read RTME, glad you liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was easily the most (body) pressing question. Thanks for the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is beautiful. You had me at the hook and put me down for the count with this fantastic romance. I really truly loved this.

(Takeru Kobayashi, Site-61 Director. May 2010 monthly communications statement, excerpt.)

You wouldn't happen to be alluding to the Takeru Koabyashi, famed competitive eater, would you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: glad i got resolution about what they do about bears

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like pulling that emotional curve ball at my readers. Glad you enjoyed it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't think I could get emotional over a town full of brawls, but here we are… It's a nice touch to an already very fun article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't hate to nitpick! You're doing a service by pointing out spelling and grammar mistakes. I've corrected these two points. Thanks for pointing them out, and thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great post! However, though I hate to nitpick, and I could be wrong, I believe these two excerpts contain errors: "but no efforts will be made to reduce the spread of it's population." (its), and "except you don’t walk down the isle; you run down it." (aisle). They really weren't a big deal, though, and I thoroughly enjoyed reading this article. Thank you for writing it! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If we're ever in the same city, weryllium, Ill be sure to punch you in the face to make you feel at home.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was ready to upvote since the moment I read the first image caption. I wanna live here now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers! As much as I enjoy the dispassionate containment-focused Foundation, it always struck me as strange that the "learning and growing" elements of scientific study are only ever mentioned in the abstract. Anomalous communities are just communities, and so long as they don't threaten the Foundations idea of normality, what's the harm?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a great exploration of how cultural exchange can sometimes flow in both directions at once…Without being boring. That's not easy to pull off.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I try to alternate between dark and light in my article posting. Last month was a sour tone town, this month it's sweet! Thanks for the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's what's so fun about the Foundation as a character. One day you can make them the coldest bastards on earth, the next they're saving kittens from trees. It's all canon. Glad you liked the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another day, another anomalous town, and all the interesting ways the world and the town interact.

I was waiting for things to go south as well, but it winds up being this adorable slice-of-life entry for the Foundation. Loved it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was 100% certain this was going to end in tragedy, with the agent mind wiped and sent to warehouse duty on the other side of the planet or something. I'm not sure if I'm relieved or disappointed. +1 in either case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you liked it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see a comment, I respond to it. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This brought a tear to my eye. Really funny, really sweet and overall just really great,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mr. Lomen has taken over as owner of the Cunningham Sawmill, and continues to reside in his two-story home with his wife and their three children.

I see a happy ending, I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, can't do everything for everyone! I'm glad you enjoyed the town details at least. Thanks for the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i really love the detail that this town still cares about safety and not bumping into stuff, despite it's nature.

not into the love story bit, but that's honestly just me, this is still rlly great. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Biggest preesh, aismallard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was fantastic to crit, and fantastic to read. Really solid piece, with steady, continuous humor throughout. The feels at the end were a great way to wrap it all up. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No I hear you. It's a bit quippy and bloated, but I tried to match the ridiculousness of the article. Thanks for the read nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dialogue could use some tightening. Some parts just… feel off for some reason. I can't put my finger on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an adorable SCP and I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn. You right. I botched a Dune reference. I better just scrap the whole article.

(low key thanks tho).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can’t agree with gee here. “Arakis” is supposed to have two r’s.

this is low key fabulous tho

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Cheers, Gee! Glad you liked it :)
T Rutherford, you’ve done it again. Everything about this is excellent.

item.title: BROOK TOWN BEAT DOWN
item.content:encoded:
JONESY: You looking for a tilly, buddy? - Let's have a donnybrook!
DARYL: Pump the brakes. You take your shirt off, but leave your sunglasses on?
WAYNE: What sort of backwards fuckin' pageantry is that?
DARYL: Do you want to fight with those shades or play pokerstars.com?

Letterkenny, 2016, Season 1 Episode 1. 'Aint no Reason to get Excited.

Earlier this month, I went to a special theatrical screening of one of my favorite movies: The Warriors. It might have inspired this article a bit.

Thanks to the critters:

Deadly Bread
Oboebandgeek99
Tanhony

And

Special thanks to aismallard, who seems to have unlimited patience for my nonsense.

Images can be found Here. The second image is also from wikicommons, but the link it saved at work. Ill update this on Tuesday!

Update: New image! Used with photographer / owners permission. Thanks Ghost1-1!

► More T Rutherford Articles and Tales ◄
